source.title: Deconstructing the human algorithms for exploration

confidence_rationale: Most directly on-topic for Q-043. Gershman shows that uncertainty-bonus algorithms (state-dependent shift, MECH-314 analog) and sampling/Thompson-style randomisation algorithms (state-independent shift, MECH-313 analog) make distinguishable predictions: uncertainty bonuses shift response BIAS as a function of uncertainty, whereas sampling shifts response SLOPE (decision noise). Two behavioural experiments find evidence for BOTH bias and slope changes, and a hybrid model is the best quantitative account. Direct positive evidence that the calibration zone Q-043 hypothesises exists — both mechanisms are needed for human-level behavioural fit.
failure_signatures: Gershman's experiments use simple two-armed bandits with explicit reward/info trade-offs, not anything like SD-054 reef substrate. The hybrid-best-fit finding is at the algorithmic level; it does not pin down which (w_313, w_314) combination is optimal — it shows only that neither extreme (uncertainty-bonus-only, sampling-only) suffices.

The model comparison uses BIC/AIC, which is sensitive to parameter count; a more flexible single-mechanism model could in principle approximate the hybrid fit. Replication in further task structures would strengthen the inference.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Algorithms based on uncertainty bonuses (e.g. UCB) and algorithms based on sampling (e.g. Thompson sampling, softmax with decision noise) make distinguishable predictions for human choice: uncertainty bonuses produce a response-BIAS shift with uncertainty, whereas sampling produces a response-SLOPE shift. Empirically humans show BOTH shifts, and a hybrid model combining both mechanisms fits best.
mapping.ree_translation: Gershman 2018 is the most direct empirical support for Q-043's PASS-as-question-resolution outcome: a Pareto frontier with non-zero contribution from BOTH mechanisms. The bias-vs-slope signature gives a clean behavioural diagnostic that the V3 sweep could use to verify whether MECH-313 and MECH-314 are operating as expected — if MECH-314-OFF produces a slope change without bias change (or vice versa), the substrate would be implementing one mechanism in the role of the other. The hybrid-best-fit finding tells us that REE should not expect calibration to converge on either extreme (w_313=0 or w_314=0).
mapping.mapping_caveat: Gershman's experiments are short-horizon two-armed-bandit tasks; the bias/slope diagnostic may not have the same signature in the long-horizon, multi-rule SD-054 reef substrate. The hybrid-best-fit claim is algorithmic-level: REE's MECH-313 noise floor is not algorithmically identical to Thompson sampling (it acts at the action-selection layer, not at the value estimation), and MECH-314's frontopolar curiosity is not algorithmically identical to UCB (it draws on the StructuredCuriosity module's per-state computation). The translation is approximate at the implementation level even though the algorithmic-class match is strong.
mapping.source_context: Computational/behavioural human studies, two experiments with n=20 and n=40 healthy adults. Two-armed bandit tasks with manipulated uncertainty about each arm's payoff. Model fits via maximum likelihood with parameter recovery.

confidence_components.notes: Cognition publication, top general-cognition venue, methodologically rigorous with parameter recovery and a clean hybrid-vs-single-mechanism model comparison. Mapping fidelity is the highest of the Q-043 set because Gershman explicitly compares the two algorithmic families that map onto MECH-313/MECH-314 and finds the hybrid wins. Transfer risk modest: human behavioural data on a simple bandit task transfers reasonably to REE's diversity-generation question.
